segments.value: To scale a vector, multiply each component by c.
A positive c keeps the direction and stretches or shrinks the length. A negative c also flips the vector to point the opposite way.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector to create 3A, then enter the resulting vector.
questions.hint: 3A multiplies each component of A by 3. Drag the head outward until c = 3 and read off ⟨3·2, 3·1⟩.
questions.explanation: 3 · ⟨2, 1⟩ = ⟨3·2, 3·1⟩ = ⟨6, 3⟩. Multiplying by a positive scalar stretches the length while the direction stays the same.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector to create −2A, then enter the resulting vector.
questions.hint: −2A multiplies each component by −2, which flips the direction and doubles the length. Drag the head through the origin until c = −2.
questions.explanation: −2 · ⟨1, 2⟩ = ⟨−2·1, −2·2⟩ = ⟨−2, −4⟩. A negative scalar reverses the direction, and |−2| = 2 doubles the length.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector until c·A matches ⟨2, 6⟩, then enter the scalar c.
questions.hint: Each component of ⟨2, 6⟩ is the same multiple of ⟨1, 3⟩. What number times 1 gives 2?

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector until c·A matches ⟨−6, 2⟩, then enter the scalar c.
questions.hint: ⟨−6, 2⟩ points opposite to A and is twice as long, so c must be negative.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector until c·A matches the target ⟨−9, −6⟩, then enter the scalar c.
questions.hint: ⟨−9, −6⟩ points opposite to A, and each component is 3 times as long. What single number times ⟨3, 2⟩ gives ⟨−9, −6⟩?
questions.explanation: c · ⟨3, 2⟩ = ⟨−9, −6⟩ means 3c = −9 and 2c = −6, so c = −3. The negative scalar flips A's direction, and |−3| = 3 triples its length.
